ks1.objective: Tag, freeze, and help your friends by freeing one of their arms.
successCriteria: Freezes with arms out when tagged
Helps free at least one teammate

lks2.objective: Practise making inclusive choices — when to help a teammate, when to run on. Each frozen player needs two different teammates to be freed.
successCriteria: Frees a teammate without being asked
Recognises when to prioritise helping over avoiding the tagger
Both arms need different rescuers — no shortcuts

uks2.objective: Use scanning, awareness and quick decisions to balance self-preservation with team responsibility while dribbling.
successCriteria: Tracks who's been frozen for longest

space.description: Large playing area suitable for the size and age of group. Use cones to clearly define the space. One or two taggers depending on numbers.

value.name: Inclusivity
value.text: Inclusivity is about choices. Every time children decide who to help, who to run past, who to go back for — they're making a choice about including others. The game gently rewards kind, inclusive choices even when running on would be quicker.

steps: Choose 1–2 taggers (bibs on). The rest are runners.
When tagged, the runner freezes standing still with both arms stretched out to the side.
To be freed: one teammate frees one arm. A DIFFERENT teammate must free the second arm.
Only when both arms are freed can the player rejoin the game.
Key rule: no one can free a frozen teammate on their own.
progressions: Begin with one tagger so the rules are clear.
Rotate taggers without changing bibs — challenges awareness (who's the tagger now?).
Add footballs — dribble while completing the game.
Add a short countdown — all stuck players must be freed before time runs out.
questions: What happens if everyone runs past a frozen teammate?
How do you decide between helping and saving yourself?
Why does it take two people to free someone?

step.space: Increase the area to challenge children physically — more space to cover, more sprints
step.task: Players must perform a simple trick or skill once they're freed before rejoining
step.equipment: Introduce safe zones where children can rest briefly
step.people: Increase number of taggers to require faster scanning and cooperation

base.showmanship: Position yourself where children and parents can see and hear you. Narrate inclusion moments by name: "Brilliant teamwork there, you didn't just help your mate — you noticed someone who needed help." This makes the value visible and audible.
